text: Hello everybody. Today I am sharing a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal recurrent umbilical hernia repair. This is a middle-aged gentleman that came to me after having a primary umbilical hernia repair for what was reported as a two centimeter hernia two or three years ago somewhere else. The recurrent hernia was only two centimeters again but causing him some pain. So we went ahead and proceeded with this case and when I do these cases I'm going into them hoping that the patient will have favorable enough peritoneum to do this. But knowing that, if necessary, I can bail out to an IPOM plus or a retro-rectus repair if maybe the hernia defect was larger.

The surgeon addresses the recurrent umbilical hernia by dissecting the preperitoneal fat and hernia sac, encountering thick, scarred tissue indicative of a recurrent condition. The hernia sac is successfully taken down, but a full-thickness burn of the umbilical skin occurs due to misidentification of tissue layers, leading to excision and reconstruction of the umbilicus. The surgeon proceeds to dissect the contralateral side, carefully navigating the preperitoneal fat and peritoneum using a cut-push technique, minimizing tension and avoiding perforations. Cold dissection is employed to complete the process before selecting and placing the mesh in the pre-peritoneal pocket.

The surgeon closes the defect in two layers using a running non-absorbable number one PDS suture. The surgeon struggles with the eighteen-inch suture length but continues to work on the inferior aspect of the pocket to ensure the fifteen by fifteen centimeter flat polypropylene uncoated mesh lays flat. Additional dissection is performed, followed by placement of eight tacking sutures, including two Vicryl sutures and three on each side, with two more centrally to secure the mesh against the fascia. A three-oh barbed suture is used to close the pocket.

So I did this case with two eight millimeter ports and one twelve millimeter port, which means I did have to close that fascial defect at the twelve millimeter site, which is always the thing that hurts the patients the most. So trying to get away from using that twelve millimeter port. However, getting into these cases and not knowing for sure that I'm going to be able to do the preperitoneal repair and therefore get the mesh down the eight millimeter trocar, I've still been putting in the twelve in case I need to do an eye palm. But thank you so much for watching. Take care.
